svg.text: REGULATORY ANALYSIS — STABLECOINS UNDER SCRUTINY: GENIUS ACT AND BRAZIL'S FRAMEWORK
Case AML-IR-2026-010 · GENIUS Act (July 2025) · FinCEN/OFAC NPRM (April 2026) · BCB Res. 519/521/561 · $319B market cap
Stablecoin AML Compliance Flow — Two Converging Frameworks
Annual Big Four for large issuers
Stablecoin AML Typologies — What I Monitor

g.text: • Issuer arbitrage: using non-PPSI stablecoins to avoid GENIUS Act controls — ACAMS 4.1 · FATF R.15
• Secondary chain-breaking: passing stablecoin through multiple VASPs to dilute originator info — FATF R.16
• Cross-border structuring: splitting transfers below jurisdictional thresholds — ACAMS 3.1.4 · FATF R.20
• Freeze-resistant routing: using stablecoins whose issuers lack demonstrated freeze capability — OFAC
• Reserve opacity: using stablecoins with undisclosed reserve structures — GENIUS Act §4 · ACAMS 4.1
Frameworks: ACAMS 4.1/4.3.3 · FATF R.15/16/20 · BSA · OFAC · GENIUS Act · BCB Res. 521 · Lei 9.613
1. Stablecoin issuer due diligence is now standard EDD — regulatory status of issuer (PPSI vs. non-PPSI) is material compliance factor
2. Cross-jurisdictional stablecoin flows require documented compliance chain — map each jurisdictional link explicitly in EDD narrative
3. Both GENIUS Act and BCB framework move in same direction — stablecoins as financial instruments with full AML obligations
Constructive direction: Bradesco + Chainalysis partnership shows Brazil institutional infrastructure is advancing alongside regulation.
The gaps are transitional. The trajectory is right. The practitioner's job is to build compliance programs adaptable to this convergence.
ACAMS CAMS 10th Ed. · FATF Recommendations · BSA/FinCEN · OFAC · GENIUS Act · BCB Res. 519/521/561 · Lei 9.613/1998

svg.text: Author: Phelipe Agnelli — AML & Blockchain Forensics · Sources: GENIUS Act · FinCEN/OFAC NPRM · BCB Res. 519/521/561 · TRM Labs · Mayer Brown · Morgan Lewis · Brookings · AML-IR-2026-010
